passages.explanation: On the first day of the week, very early in the morning, the women take the spices they've prepared and go to the tomb. They find the stone rolled away but when they enter, they don't find the body of the Lord Jesus. While they're wondering about this, two men in clothes that gleam like lightning stand beside them. The women bow to the ground in fear. The men say: why do you look for the living among the dead? He is not here — he has risen! Remember how he told you while he was still with you in Galilee: the Son of Man must be delivered over to the hands of sinners, be crucified, and on the third day be raised again. Then they remember his words. They return from the tomb and tell all these things to the Eleven and all the others. It's Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Mary the mother of James, and the others with them. But the apostles don't believe them — their words seem like nonsense. Peter, however, runs to the tomb. Bending over, he sees the strips of linen lying by themselves. He goes away, wondering to himself what has happened. The angels don't say 'he will rise' — they say 'remember what he already told you.' The resurrection isn't new information; it's the fulfilment of what Jesus said repeatedly. The women believe first. The apostles dismiss them. Peter investigates but doesn't yet understand.

That same day two of them are going to a village called Emmaus, about seven miles from Jerusalem. They're talking with each other about everything that has happened. As they talk and discuss, Jesus himself comes up and walks along with them — but they're kept from recognising him. He asks: what are you discussing? They stand still, their faces downcast. One of them, named Cleopas, asks: are you the only visitor to Jerusalem who doesn't know the things that have happened? What things? he asks. They tell him about Jesus of Nazareth — a prophet powerful in word and deed before God and all the people. The chief priests and rulers handed him over to be sentenced to death and crucified him. But we had hoped that he was the one who was going to redeem Israel. And what's more, it's the third day since all this happened. Some of our women amazed us — they went to the tomb early this morning, didn't find his body, came and told us they'd seen a vision of angels who said he was alive. Some of our companions went to the tomb and found it just as the women had said, but they didn't see Jesus. Jesus responds: how foolish you are, and how slow to believe all that the prophets have spoken! Didn't the Messiah have to suffer these things and then enter his glory? And beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, he explains to them what is said in all the Scriptures concerning himself. The entire Old Testament is a story pointing to Jesus — his suffering and glory. These two disciples knew the facts but couldn't see the pattern until Jesus himself walked them through it.

As they approach the village, Jesus continues on as if he's going further. But they urge him strongly: stay with us — it's nearly evening, the day is almost over. So he goes in to stay with them. When he's at the table with them, he takes bread, gives thanks, breaks it, and begins to give it to them. Then their eyes are opened and they recognise him — and he disappears from their sight. They ask each other: weren't our hearts burning within us while he talked with us on the road and opened the Scriptures to us? They get up and return at once to Jerusalem. There they find the Eleven and those with them, assembled together and saying: it's true! The Lord has risen and has appeared to Simon. Then the two tell what happened on the road and how Jesus was recognised by them when he broke the bread. Recognition comes in the breaking of bread — the same gesture from the Last Supper, from the feeding of the five thousand. They knew his teaching but recognised his person in a meal. And the moment they see him, he's gone. The resurrection isn't about clinging to Jesus' physical presence — it's about recognising him and then going to tell others.

While they're still talking about this, Jesus himself stands among them and says: peace be with you. They're startled and frightened, thinking they see a ghost. He says: why are you troubled, and why do doubts rise in your minds? Look at my hands and my feet — it's me. Touch me and see — a ghost doesn't have flesh and bones, as you see I have. When he shows them his hands and feet, they still can't believe it because of joy and amazement. He asks: do you have anything here to eat? They give him a piece of broiled fish, and he eats it in their presence. Jesus goes out of his way to prove his physical resurrection. He's not a spirit or hallucination — he has wounds you can touch and a body that eats fish. The resurrection isn't escape from the body; it's the transformation of the body. Luke notes they disbelieved 'because of joy' — sometimes the good news is too good to process immediately.

Jesus says: this is what I told you while I was still with you — everything must be fulfilled that is written about me in the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms. Then he opens their minds so they can understand the Scriptures. He tells them: this is what is written — the Messiah will suffer and rise from the dead on the third day, and repentance for the forgiveness of sins will be preached in his name to all nations, beginning at Jerusalem. You are witnesses of these things. I'm going to send you what my Father has promised — but stay in the city until you've been clothed with power from on high. Jesus divides Scripture into three sections (Law, Prophets, Psalms — the Jewish Tanakh) and says all three point to him. The commission is global: all nations, starting from Jerusalem. But they must wait for the Holy Spirit — the power comes before the mission. This sets up Acts chapter 1 directly.

Jesus leads them out to the vicinity of Bethany. He lifts up his hands and blesses them. While he's blessing them, he leaves them and is taken up into heaven. They worship him and return to Jerusalem with great joy. And they stay continually at the temple, praising God. Luke's Gospel ends where it began — in the temple. But everything has changed. Zechariah entered the temple in fear and left unable to speak. The disciples enter the temple in joy, praising God without restraint. The story that began with a barren couple in Jerusalem ends with a community bursting with life in the same city, waiting for the power that will send them to the ends of the earth.
